metadata.file_role: Chanterelles, trumpets, toothed fungi, clubs, and corals
metadata.purpose: Non-gilled fleshy fungi grouped by stable field morphology.
metadata.organization_rule: Physical file placement follows stable biological or morphological identity. Useful parts, routes, safety, and seasonal behavior remain record-level fields.

records.notes: Cascading white tooth fungus with branched clusters rather than one round pom-pom mass.
records.medicinal_uses: Hericium species have modern supplement interest, but this entry is treated primarily as a food mushroom.
records.culinary_uses: Choice edible when young and white. Cook like lion's mane or other Hericium: sauté, brown in butter, or use in rich mushroom dishes.
records.general_notes: One of the Hericium mushrooms; useful to distinguish from lion's mane and coral tooth.
records.edibility_detail: Good edible Hericium when young, white, and tender after confident identification. Confirm a branched white fruitbody with long teeth hanging from branch tips on hardwood; avoid yellowing, brown, soggy, or decaying specimens. Photo coverage remains below the 2-image goal until another exact-rights image is verified.

mushroom_profile.season_note: Late summer through fall on hardwood logs and trunks.

records.field_identification: Branched white tooth fungus with longer teeth clustered on branch tips; found on hardwood logs or wounds.
records.mushroom_card_note: Choice Hericium: branched white clusters with hanging teeth, usually on hardwood.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Hericium americanum
use_links.notes: Branching Hericium traits and hardwood habitat.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Hericium overview
use_links.notes: Separates lion's mane, bear's head tooth, and coral tooth forms.

records.look_alike_notes: Compared with lion's mane and coral tooth, bear's head tooth is branched but has longer, shaggy clusters of teeth hanging from branch tips.

lookalike_risk.label: Moderate — edible Hericium confusion / condition risk
lookalike_risk.short_label: Moderate — edible Hericium confusion / condition risk
lookalike_risk.summary: Hericium risk is mostly edible species confusion and condition: verify white toothed growth on hardwood and reject yellowed/soggy/old material.
required_checks: Check branching/tooth arrangement if species-level matters
Reject yellowed, brown, soggy, or old specimens
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: No teeth, no Hericium; use Hericium group-level if species separation is uncertain.

target_traits: Wood-growing Hericium mass with hanging teeth
lookalike_traits: Terrestrial cap-and-stem mushroom with teeth under a cap
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Cap-and-stem teeth on soil = Hydnum-style hedgehog, not Hericium.

lookalike_traits: Upright coral branches without hanging teeth
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: No teeth, no Hericium.

records.notes: Dark hollow trumpet with no true gills; the outside fertile surface is smooth to wrinkled, not bladed.
records.culinary_uses: Choice edible with deep, aromatic flavor. Excellent fresh or dried; use in sauces, soups, eggs, rice, or anywhere a concentrated mushroom flavor helps.
records.general_notes: Easy to miss because of its dark color, especially in leaf litter.
records.edibility_detail: Choice edible when fresh and confidently identified by hollow trumpet shape, thin flesh, and lack of true gills. Clean carefully; grit and leaf litter hide inside the trumpet.

mushroom_profile.spore_print: Pale pinkish orange to yellowish orange
mushroom_profile.season_note: Spring through fall regionally; app window remains July through October for Upper Michigan practicality.
mushroom_profile.common_name_note: Often called black trumpet; older sources may use Craterellus cornucopioides.
mushroom_profile.underside_type: Smooth to shallowly wrinkled outer fertile surface; no true gills
mushroom_profile.scientific_name: Craterellus fallax / eastern black trumpet group
mushroom_profile.entry_scope: eastern North American species / tight group
mushroom_profile.summary: Thin hollow dark trumpet with no true gills and a smooth to shallowly wrinkled outer fertile surface.
mushroom_profile.host_certainty: oak association strong; other hardwood associations possible

records.scientific_name: Craterellus fallax / eastern black trumpet group
records.foraging_value: Choice edible when fresh and confidently identified
records.field_identification: Thin, hollow, deeply vase-shaped mushroom with a gray-black inner surface and a dark outer surface that is smooth to very shallowly wrinkled, never blade-gilled. The outer surface can develop yellowish or orange tones as pale pinkish-orange spores mature.
records.mushroom_card_note: Eastern hollow black trumpet with no true gills and a pale pinkish-orange print.

images_structured.author: R. DN.

images_structured.title: Black trumpet cluster
images_structured.author: R. DN.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Craterellus fallax
use_links.notes: Eastern North American black trumpet; distinguishes its pale pinkish-orange print from the white-print European species.

records.look_alike_notes: Few dangerous look-alikes when the hollow trumpet shape and lack of true gills are confirmed. Do not confuse with blackened rotten mushrooms, dark cup fungi, or random decayed funnel-shaped mushrooms. Black trumpets should be thin, hollow, trumpet-shaped, and fresh, not slimy or decomposing.
records.bad_condition_warnings: Reject slimy, rotten, sour-smelling, collapsed, or blackened mushrooms that are not naturally trumpet-shaped and hollow. Check inside for insects, grit, and debris. Drying quality is good, but only start with clean, fresh specimens.
records.image_needs: Needs habitat cluster in leaf litter/moss, side profile showing hollow trumpet, inside of trumpet, outer fertile surface showing wrinkles/no gills, cross-section, and spore print if available.
records.quality_notes: Small but powerful flavor; often dirty because of the vase shape.
records.eating_use_notes: Excellent dried and powdered; clean carefully.
records.risk_reason: Main risk is confirmation of the true trumpet/vase structure and condition, not food quality.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Dark vase or trumpet shape can collect grit and leaf litter; reject rotten, waterlogged, or uncertain dark mushrooms.
records.hard_rule: Use only confirmed black trumpets in good condition; do not treat every dark funnel-shaped mushroom as food.

lookalike_risk.label: Low — no major dangerous look-alike flagged
lookalike_risk.summary: No close deadly or strongly poisonous look-alike is currently flagged for this app entry. Still confirm the full ID and condition.
required_checks: Confirm the app’s field marks and growth habit
Reject old, rotten, buggy, or uncertain specimens
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Low look-alike risk does not mean skip identification.

edible_use.notes: Clean the hollow center carefully and cook; dry sound specimens for concentrated flavor.

records.source_note: v4.3.208 adds exact source/use fields and normalizes the eastern pale-pinkish-orange spore print.

records.notes: Large ruffled mass with noodle-like folds rather than gills, pores, or teeth.
records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Use young, clean specimens. Slice or tear into ribbons after careful cleaning; good sautéed, in soups, or dried if clean enough.
records.general_notes: Eastern North American cauliflower mushrooms are often treated around the Sparassis spathulata/americana complex. Use as a field-group entry rather than overpromising one species name.
records.edibility_detail: Good edible when young, clean, and confidently identified. Dirt, bugs, and sand collect deep in the folds, so discard old, waterlogged, or hard-to-clean specimens.

underside: Wrinkled folds; no gills or pores
substrate: Near conifers or hardwoods depending species

records.field_identification: Large cream to pale yellow mass of flattened, ruffled, noodle-like folds at the base of trees or from buried roots. It looks like a loose cauliflower or egg noodles, not an upright coral with many tiny branch tips.
records.mushroom_card_note: Ruffled noodle-like mass at tree bases; good when young and clean, but it can be a dirt-and-bug hotel.

edible_use.notes: Good edible when young, clean, and confidently identified. Dirt, bugs, and sand collect deep in the folds, so discard old, waterlogged, or hard-to-clean specimens.

lookalike_risk.label: Moderate — coral/rosette confusion
lookalike_risk.summary: Cauliflower mushroom needs noodle/ribbon-like folds, soft pliable flesh, and root/wood association, not upright brittle coral branches.
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Brittle upright coral branches are not cauliflower mushroom.

dangerous_lookalikes.display_name: Pale coral fungi / false coral-type mushrooms
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Brittle upright coral branches are not cauliflower mushroom.

lookalike_traits: Pored fan fronds in gray-brown rosette
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Pores on fronds route to hen/polypore, not Sparassis.

lookalike_traits: Separate little caps on branching stalks
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Umbrella-like caps are not cauliflower.

source_review_notes: Route remains edible-with-caution/prep-required. Current MushroomExpert links support ID/taxonomy/comparison; add explicit edible-use/prep/cleaning support in a future source-hygiene pass.

records.notes: White coral-like tooth fungus with many branches carrying hanging spines.
records.medicinal_uses: Hericium species have medicinal/supplement interest, but this entry is treated primarily as food.
records.culinary_uses: Choice edible when young and white. Cook gently; older yellowing specimens lose quality.
records.general_notes: Another Hericium ally that benefits from side-by-side comparison with lion's mane and bear's head tooth.
records.edibility_detail: Good edible Hericium when young, white, and tender after confident identification. Confirm short teeth arranged along delicate branching structures on dead hardwood; avoid yellowing, brittle, dirty, or decaying specimens and separate it from bear’s head tooth and lion’s mane by branching and tooth placement.

mushroom_profile.season_note: Late summer through fall on hardwood logs.

records.field_identification: Branched white Hericium with short teeth hanging in rows along the branches, usually on dead hardwood.
records.mushroom_card_note: Choice toothed edible; short teeth hang along delicate branches on hardwood.

records.look_alike_notes: Coral tooth has many branches with teeth along the branches; bear's head tooth has longer teeth clustered near branch tips, while lion's mane is a denser unbranched clump.

records.notes: Instead of gills or pores, these have short dangling teeth under the cap.
records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Trim dirty tooth layer if needed. Good sautéed or added to mixed mushroom dishes; texture stays firmer than many gilled mushrooms.
records.general_notes: One of the friendlier edible mushrooms for beginners because true teeth are easier to read than subtle gill attachment.
records.edibility_detail: Choice edible when young and confidently identified by the tooth underside. Older specimens can become bitter or buggy.

records.field_identification: Pale buff to orange-tan cap with blunt, removable teeth under the cap instead of gills or pores. Teeth run slightly down the stem; flesh is firm and brittle. The tooth underside is the deal-breaker ID feature.
records.mushroom_card_note: Look underneath: blunt hanging teeth, not gills. Choice edible when young.

edible_use.notes: Choice edible when young and confidently identified by the tooth underside. Older specimens can become bitter or buggy.

lookalike_risk.label: Moderate — tooth/gill/quality confusion
lookalike_risk.summary: Hedgehogs are comparatively safe when the underside has soft teeth/spines, but underside confirmation is mandatory.
required_checks: Reject ridges, gills, or dark scaly toothed mushrooms

dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: No teeth = not a hedgehog.
Wood-growing tooth mass routes to Hericium, not Hydnum.

target_traits: Pale peach/tan Hydnum with soft teeth
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Dark scaly toothed mushrooms are not beginner hedgehogs.

records.medicinal_uses: Popular medicinal-interest species, especially for nerve/cognition claims, but this guide should treat claims as preliminary/traditional unless supported by specific research.
records.culinary_uses: Tear or slice fresh white flesh and cook thoroughly; often used like seafood-textured mushroom in sautés or patties.
records.general_notes: Separate from bear’s head tooth and coral tooth by growth form: lion’s mane is mostly unbranched with one compact mass of teeth.
records.edibility_detail: Choice edible when fresh, white, and confidently identified. Avoid yellowed, soggy, or old specimens.
records.field_identification: Single unbranched white pom-pom or beard of long dangling teeth, usually from a wound on living hardwood or from recently dead hardwood. It lacks the branched coral-like structure of other Hericium species.
records.mushroom_card_note: White unbranched pom-pom of dangling teeth on hardwood; choice food, medicinal-interest claims stay cautious.

records.culinary_uses: Cook like other chanterelles; best used fresh because small fruitbodies dry out quickly.
records.general_notes: A small eastern hardwood chanterelle documented from Michigan. Color is distinctive, but the decisive checks are ground growth, blunt decurrent false gills, firm pale flesh, and the absence of true blade-like gills.

records.field_identification: Small flamingo-pink to cinnabar-red chanterelle on the ground in hardwood forests. The underside has well-spaced, blunt false gills that run down the stem, often fork and cross-vein, and are the same color as the cap or slightly paler. The stem is dry and cap-colored, the flesh is whitish and does not change when cut, the odor is sweet or fragrant, and the spore print is whitish to slightly pinkish.
records.edibility_detail: Choice edible when confidently identified by false gills/ridges, ground growth, firm flesh, and appropriate habitat. Do not eat orange mushrooms with true blade-like gills or mushrooms growing in dense clusters from wood or buried roots.
records.mushroom_card_note: Small red chanterelle with well-spaced blunt false gills; grows from soil under hardwoods.

use_links.label: MSU: Cantharellus cinnabarinus
use_links.notes: Michigan/Great Lakes context for cinnabar chanterelles.

edible_use.notes: Good edible after confirming blunt false gills/ridges and chanterelle structure. Avoid orange gilled mushrooms on wood, especially jack-o-lanterns.

records.look_alike_notes: Main concern is jack-o’-lantern mushrooms. Jack-o’-lanterns have true blade-like gills, often grow in dense clusters from wood, stumps, buried roots, or tree bases, and are typically deeper pumpkin-orange. Chanterelles grow from the ground and have blunt ridges or false gills rather than separable true gills. False chanterelles and other orange gilled mushrooms should also be separated by true gills, softer texture, and different growth habits.
records.bad_condition_warnings: Reject slimy, brown-edged, waterlogged, moldy, or bug-heavy specimens. Reject specimens where the underside has sharp, separable true gills. Reject orange clustered mushrooms from wood, stumps, or buried roots.
records.image_needs: Needs underside macro showing false gills/ridges, side view showing ridges running down stem, split specimen showing pale/solid flesh, habitat shot showing ground growth, and chanterelle vs jack-o’-lantern comparison.
records.quality_notes: High-value table mushrooms when firm, fresh, and not waterlogged.
records.eating_use_notes: Cook fresh; useful sautéed, dried, or preserved depending on species.
records.risk_reason: The main danger is confusing true chanterelles with jack-o’-lanterns or false chanterelles. Jack-o’-lanterns are poisonous.
records.symptoms_body_systems: If confused with jack-o’-lantern: gastrointestinal tract, with cramps, vomiting, and diarrhea.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: True chanterelles have blunt, forked ridges or false gills that look melted into the underside, grow from soil, and are usually singly or loosely scattered. Jack-o’-lanterns grow in dense clusters from wood or buried roots and have sharp true gills.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat a red or orange mushroom as a chanterelle unless the underside has blunt, decurrent false gills rather than thin separable blades and the mushroom is growing from soil, not wood.
records.caution_reason: The main danger is confusing true chanterelles with jack-o’-lanterns or false chanterelles. Jack-o’-lanterns are poisonous.

lookalike_risk.label: High — orange/red gilled look-alike possible
lookalike_risk.summary: Cinnabar chanterelles need the same underside/substrate checks as yellow chanterelles, with small red/cinnabar traits.
required_checks: Confirm blunt ridges or shallow folds
Confirm ground growth and pale inner flesh
Reject sharp true gills and wood clusters
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Do not eat small red/orange mushrooms as chanterelles unless the underside has blunt ridges/folds and the mushroom grows from soil.

target_traits: Blunt forked ridges, wrinkles, folds, or nearly smooth underside
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Wood clusters with sharp true orange gills are not chanterelles.
Sharp separable true gills mean do not eat as chanterelle.

records.culinary_uses: Cook fresh like other chanterelles; good sautéed, in cream sauces, eggs, or dried if clean.
records.general_notes: Treat this as the smooth-chanterelle group in a field guide. North American smooth chanterelles show substantial variation, and species-level separation may require more than casual field characters.
records.field_identification: Medium to large yellow-orange chanterelle with a smooth to shallowly wrinkled underside rather than developed blade-like gills. It grows from soil with oaks and sometimes hickories. The cap becomes shallowly vase-shaped, the stout stem tapers toward the base, the white flesh stays unchanged when cut, bruised surfaces may slowly turn yellowish to orangish brown, the odor is often strongly apricot-like, and the spore print is pale pinkish yellow.
records.mushroom_card_note: Smooth chanterelle: underside smooth to shallowly wrinkled, not truly gilled; usually with oak.

edible_use.notes: Choice edible after confirming chanterelle structure, soil growth, solid flesh, and lack of true sharp gills. Avoid orange gilled clusters on wood.

records.look_alike_notes: Main concern is jack-o’-lantern mushrooms. Jack-o’-lanterns have true blade-like gills, often grow in dense clusters from wood, stumps, buried roots, or tree bases, and are typically deeper pumpkin-orange. Chanterelles grow from the ground and have blunt ridges or false gills rather than separable true gills. False chanterelles and other orange gilled mushrooms should also be separated by true gills, softer texture, and different growth habits.
records.bad_condition_warnings: Reject slimy, brown-edged, waterlogged, moldy, or bug-heavy specimens. Reject specimens where the underside has sharp, separable true gills. Reject orange clustered mushrooms from wood, stumps, or buried roots.
records.image_needs: Needs underside macro showing false gills/ridges, side view showing ridges running down stem, split specimen showing pale/solid flesh, habitat shot showing ground growth, and chanterelle vs jack-o’-lantern comparison.

records.eating_use_notes: Cook fresh; useful sautéed, dried, or preserved depending on species.
records.risk_reason: The main danger is confusing true chanterelles with jack-o’-lanterns or false chanterelles. Jack-o’-lanterns are poisonous.
records.symptoms_body_systems: If confused with jack-o’-lantern: gastrointestinal tract, with cramps, vomiting, and diarrhea.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: True chanterelles have blunt, forked ridges or false gills that look melted into the underside, grow from soil, and are usually singly or loosely scattered. Jack-o’-lanterns grow in dense clusters from wood or buried roots and have sharp true gills.
records.hard_rule: A nearly smooth underside can still be a chanterelle, but thin separable blade-like gills or clustered growth from wood rule this record out.
records.caution_reason: The main danger is confusing true chanterelles with jack-o’-lanterns or false chanterelles. Jack-o’-lanterns are poisonous.

lookalike_risk.label: High — poisonous orange-gilled look-alike possible
lookalike_risk.summary: Chanterelles must be separated from jack-o’-lanterns and false chanterelles by underside, flesh, and substrate.
required_checks: Confirm blunt forked ridges, wrinkles, folds, or nearly smooth underside
Confirm ground growth, not dense wood clusters
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: For yellow/smooth chanterelles, sharp true gills or dense orange wood clusters mean stop.

target_traits: Blunt forked ridges, wrinkles, folds, or nearly smooth underside
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Wood clusters with sharp true orange gills are not chanterelles.
Sharp separable true gills mean do not eat as chanterelle.

records.culinary_uses: Choice edible chanterelle-type mushrooms when positively identified. Cook; do not confuse with jack-o'-lanterns or true-gilled orange mushrooms.
records.general_notes: Do not label eastern North American yellow chanterelles as European Cantharellus cibarius. This record intentionally stays at complex level because several eastern species overlap strongly in ordinary field appearance.
records.field_identification: Yellow to orange-yellow chanterelle-type mushrooms growing from soil in conifer-associated woods. The underside has blunt, forked, often cross-veined false gills that run down the stem and look molded into the flesh rather than forming thin separable blades. The stem is solid and fleshy, the interior is white to pale yellow, and the odor may be fruity or apricot-like. Exact eastern North American species can require a thick spore print, careful false-gill color, host-tree notes, microscopy, or DNA.
records.mushroom_card_note: Yellow chanterelle complex with conifers; blunt false gills, firm pale flesh, and soil growth.

staining: Species-dependent; document any browning or darkening
mushroom_profile.spore_print: Species-dependent: white, cream, yellow, pinkish, or salmon
key_filters: Thick spore print needed for finer separation

records.edibility_detail: Choice edible when confidently identified by false gills/ridges, ground growth, firm flesh, and appropriate habitat. Do not eat orange mushrooms with true blade-like gills or mushrooms growing in dense clusters from wood or buried roots.

records.look_alike_notes: Main concern is jack-o’-lantern mushrooms. Jack-o’-lanterns have true blade-like gills, often grow in dense clusters from wood, stumps, buried roots, or tree bases, and are typically deeper pumpkin-orange. Chanterelles grow from the ground and have blunt ridges or false gills rather than separable true gills. False chanterelles and other orange gilled mushrooms should also be separated by true gills, softer texture, and different growth habits.
records.bad_condition_warnings: Reject slimy, brown-edged, waterlogged, moldy, or bug-heavy specimens. Reject specimens where the underside has sharp, separable true gills. Reject orange clustered mushrooms from wood, stumps, or buried roots.
records.image_needs: Needs underside macro showing false gills/ridges, side view showing ridges running down stem, split specimen showing pale/solid flesh, habitat shot showing ground growth, and chanterelle vs jack-o’-lantern comparison.
records.quality_notes: High-value table mushrooms when firm, fresh, and not waterlogged.
records.eating_use_notes: Cook fresh; useful sautéed, dried, or preserved depending on species.
records.risk_reason: The main danger is confusing true chanterelles with jack-o’-lanterns or false chanterelles. Jack-o’-lanterns are poisonous.
records.symptoms_body_systems: If confused with jack-o’-lantern: gastrointestinal tract, with cramps, vomiting, and diarrhea.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: True chanterelles have blunt, forked ridges or false gills that look melted into the underside, grow from soil, and are usually singly or loosely scattered. Jack-o’-lanterns grow in dense clusters from wood or buried roots and have sharp true gills.
records.hard_rule: Use only firm, ground-growing chanterelles with blunt false gills. Thin blade-like gills or clustered growth from wood or buried roots rule the mushroom out.
records.caution_reason: The main danger is confusing true chanterelles with jack-o’-lanterns or false chanterelles. Jack-o’-lanterns are poisonous.

lookalike_risk.label: High — poisonous orange-gilled look-alike possible
lookalike_risk.summary: Chanterelles must be separated from jack-o’-lanterns and false chanterelles by underside, flesh, and substrate.
required_checks: Confirm blunt forked ridges, wrinkles, folds, or nearly smooth underside
Confirm ground growth, not dense wood clusters
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: For yellow/smooth chanterelles, sharp true gills or dense orange wood clusters mean stop.

target_traits: Blunt forked ridges, wrinkles, folds, or nearly smooth underside
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Wood clusters with sharp true orange gills are not chanterelles.
Sharp separable true gills mean do not eat as chanterelle.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: eastern Cantharellus “cibarius” group
use_links.notes: Explains eastern North American species complexity, false-gill color, host documentation, and variable spore-print colors.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Chanterelles and trumpets
use_links.notes: Family-level distinction between smooth, wrinkled, false-gilled surfaces and true blade-like gills.

records.culinary_uses: Choice edible chanterelle-type mushrooms when positively identified. Cook; check false gills, growth from soil, and lack of jack-o'-lantern clustering on wood.
records.general_notes: Do not label eastern North American yellow chanterelles as European Cantharellus cibarius. This record intentionally stays at complex level because several eastern species overlap strongly in ordinary field appearance.
records.field_identification: Yellow to orange-yellow chanterelle-type mushrooms growing from soil in hardwood-associated woods. The underside has blunt, forked, often cross-veined false gills that run down the stem and look molded into the flesh rather than forming thin separable blades. The stem is solid and fleshy, the interior is white to pale yellow, and the odor may be fruity or apricot-like. Exact eastern North American species can require a thick spore print, careful false-gill color, host-tree notes, microscopy, or DNA.
records.mushroom_card_note: Yellow chanterelle complex with hardwoods; blunt false gills, firm pale flesh, and soil growth.

staining: Species-dependent; document any browning or darkening
mushroom_profile.spore_print: Species-dependent: white, cream, yellow, pinkish, or salmon
key_filters: Thick spore print needed for finer separation

records.edibility_detail: Choice edible when confidently identified by false gills/ridges, ground growth, firm flesh, and appropriate habitat. Do not eat orange mushrooms with true blade-like gills or mushrooms growing in dense clusters from wood or buried roots.

records.culinary_uses: Good fresh or dried; small mushrooms dry well and rehydrate into soups and sauces.
records.general_notes: The yellow hollow stem, brown perforated cap, mossy conifer-bog habitat, and white spore print are more useful than the common name. Eastern and western North American yellowfoot populations may not all represent the same species, so this remains a cautious eastern group record.
records.field_identification: Small late-season chanterelle relative from mossy northern conifer bogs and very well-decayed moss-covered wood. The brown to blackish-brown cap becomes vase-shaped and often develops a central opening. The underside has yellowish to grayish, forked, cross-veined false gills running down the stem. The thin stem is waxy, yellow to orange-yellow, and becomes hollow. Flesh is thin and brownish to yellowish, and the spore print is white.
records.mushroom_card_note: Late-season yellowfoot: brown perforated cap, forked false gills, hollow yellow stem, mossy conifer habitat.

mushroom_profile.growth_from_wood: Sometimes from thoroughly decayed moss-covered wood
stem_color: Dull yellow to brownish with age
mushroom_profile.season_note: Fall, often late in the season.

records.edibility_detail: Choice edible when confidently identified by false gills/ridges, ground growth, firm flesh, and appropriate habitat. Do not eat orange mushrooms with true blade-like gills or mushrooms growing in dense clusters from wood or buried roots.

edible_use.notes: Good edible after confirming blunt decurrent ridges, hollow yellowish stem, and soil/moss habitat. Avoid small brown gilled mushrooms with true gills.

records.look_alike_notes: Main concern is jack-o’-lantern mushrooms. Jack-o’-lanterns have true blade-like gills, often grow in dense clusters from wood, stumps, buried roots, or tree bases, and are typically deeper pumpkin-orange. Chanterelles grow from the ground and have blunt ridges or false gills rather than separable true gills. False chanterelles and other orange gilled mushrooms should also be separated by true gills, softer texture, and different growth habits.
records.bad_condition_warnings: Reject slimy, brown-edged, waterlogged, moldy, or bug-heavy specimens. Reject specimens where the underside has sharp, separable true gills. Reject orange clustered mushrooms from wood, stumps, or buried roots.

records.field_traits_that_create_risk: True chanterelles have blunt, forked ridges or false gills that look melted into the underside, grow from soil, and are usually singly or loosely scattered. Jack-o’-lanterns grow in dense clusters from wood or buried roots and have sharp true gills.
records.hard_rule: Do not use this record for a solid-stemmed yellow chanterelle or any mushroom with thin true gills. Confirm the hollow waxy yellow stem and forked, cross-veined false gills.
records.caution_reason: The main danger is confusing true chanterelles with jack-o’-lanterns or false chanterelles. Jack-o’-lanterns are poisonous.

lookalike_risk.label: Moderate — small orange/brown mushroom confusion possible
lookalike_risk.short_label: Moderate — small orange/brown mushroom confusion possible
lookalike_risk.summary: Yellowfoot is less like jack-o’-lantern than golden chanterelles, but it still needs the hollow yellow stem and fold-like underside package.
required_checks: Confirm blunt folds/shallow ridges, not sharp gills
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Do not use yellow color alone; no hollow yellow stem and fold-like underside means no yellowfoot.

dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Sharp true gills mean not yellowfoot.

dangerous_lookalikes.warning: Orange true-gilled clusters from wood are not the same as edible shelf/ridge mushrooms.
target_traits: Target mushroom lacks true orange gills
lookalike_traits: Dense clusters from wood, roots, or buried roots
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: If it has true orange gills, route away from the edible target.
